sections.java: // Parens + braces required if (score >= 90) { grade = "A"; } else if (score >= 80) { grade = "B"; } else { grade = "C"; }
sections.python: # Colon + indentation, elif keyword if score >= 90: grade = "A" elif score >= 80: grade = "B" else: grade = "C"

sections.title: No Ternary Operator — Use the Conditional Expression
sections.content: Python has no cond ? a : b. The equivalent reads like English and flips the order: grade = "A" if score >= 90 else "B". The value-if-true comes first, then the condition, then the else value.

sections.java: // Index-based or enhanced for for (int i = 0; i < items.size(); i++) { System.out.println(i + ": " + items.get(i)); } for (String item : items) { process(item); }
sections.python: # for-in iterates values directly for item in items: process(item) # Need the index too? enumerate() for i, item in enumerate(items): print(f"{i}: {item}")

sections.title: enumerate(), zip(), and range()
sections.content: Forget C-style index loops. enumerate(seq) gives (index, value) pairs. zip(a, b) walks two sequences in lockstep. range(5) is 0..4, range(2, 10, 2) steps by 2. These three cover nearly every loop you'd ever write a counter for in Java.

sections.java: // Modern switch (Java 17+ pattern matching) String kind = switch (shape) { case Circle c -> "round"; case Square s -> "boxy"; default -> "unknown"; };
sections.python: # match/case — structural pattern matching (3.10+) match shape: case Circle(): kind = "round" case Square(): kind = "boxy" case _: kind = "unknown"

sections.title: match Is More Powerful Than switch
sections.content: Python's match can destructure: case Point(x=0, y=y) binds y while matching x == 0. It matches sequences case [first, *rest] and dicts case {"role": "admin"}. The wildcard case _ is the default. There's no fall-through, so no break needed.

sections.java: // for-else has no equivalent — use a flag boolean found = false; for (String s : items) { if (s.equals(target)) { found = true; break; } } if (!found) { handleMissing(); }
sections.python: # for-else: else runs if loop did NOT break for s in items: if s == target: handle(s) break else: handle_missing() # no break happened

sections.title: Truthiness: Empty Collections Are Falsy
sections.content: Python treats 0, "", [], {}, and None as falsy. So if items: means "if the list is non-empty" — idiomatic and preferred over if len(items) > 0. But beware: if x: when x could legitimately be 0 may skip valid data. Use if x is not None: when you mean "present".

sections.question: What does `"A" if score >= 90 else "B"` correspond to in Java?
options: An if-else statement that must be on separate lines
Nothing — Python can't do inline conditionals
sections.explanation: It's Python's conditional expression, the direct analog of Java's ternary score >= 90 ? "A" : "B" — but written value-first: the result if true, then if condition, then else result.
